title: Construction Injuries
description: Construction sites are among the most hazardous work environments. Workers injured on a job site may have options beyond workers' compensation — including third-party claims against general contractors, subcontractors, equipment manufacturers, and property owners.
metaTitle: Construction Injury Guide — Workers' Comp vs. Third-Party Claims
metaDescription: What to do after a construction site injury in California or Arizona. Understand workers' comp, third-party liability, OSHA reporting, and your rights.

commonCauses.title: Falls from Heights
commonCauses.description: Falls from scaffolding, ladders, roofs, and elevated platforms are the leading cause of construction fatalities and serious injuries.

commonCauses.title: Struck-By Accidents
commonCauses.description: Workers struck by falling tools, equipment, or vehicles on active job sites. Overhead work creates significant struck-by hazards.

commonCauses.title: Caught-In/Between Accidents
commonCauses.description: Workers caught in or between heavy equipment, machinery, or collapsing structures. These accidents are frequently fatal or catastrophic.

commonCauses.title: Electrocution
commonCauses.description: Contact with live wires, inadequate lockout/tagout procedures, and proximity to power lines are leading electrical hazards on construction sites.

commonCauses.title: Defective Equipment or Tools
commonCauses.description: Malfunctioning power tools, defective scaffolding components, and faulty equipment can cause serious injuries independent of worker error.

commonCauses.title: Trench and Excavation Collapse
commonCauses.description: Unprotected trenches and excavations can collapse suddenly, burying workers. OSHA has specific shoring and protective system requirements.

commonCauses.title: Exposure to Hazardous Materials
commonCauses.description: Asbestos, silica dust, lead, and other construction materials pose serious long-term health risks without adequate protective equipment.

immediateSteps.title: Get emergency medical care
immediateSteps.description: Construction injuries can be severe. Call 911 or have a coworker call immediately. Do not downplay symptoms.

immediateSteps.title: Report the injury to your employer immediately
immediateSteps.description: Notify your employer or site supervisor as soon as possible. This is required to preserve workers' compensation rights in both California and Arizona.

immediateSteps.title: Document the scene before it changes
immediateSteps.description: Construction sites change rapidly. Photograph the exact location of the accident, the equipment involved, any defects, and safety violations before the site is modified.

immediateSteps.title: Identify witnesses
immediateSteps.description: Names and contact information of coworkers or others who witnessed the accident.

immediateSteps.title: Preserve any defective equipment
immediateSteps.description: If a tool or piece of equipment failed, preserve it as evidence. Do not allow it to be repaired, discarded, or returned.

immediateSteps.title: Document any OSHA violations observed
immediateSteps.description: Note any safety requirements that were not followed at the time of the accident. OSHA violations can support third-party liability claims.

immediateSteps.title: File a workers' compensation claim
immediateSteps.description: File with your employer's workers' comp carrier promptly. Missing reporting deadlines can jeopardize your benefits.

immediateSteps.title: Keep records of everything
immediateSteps.description: All medical bills, lost wage documentation, and communications with your employer, insurer, or any third parties.

items: Photos of the exact accident location
Photos of any equipment, tools, or materials involved
Photos of any safety violations or missing safeguards
Names and contact info of witnesses
Preserve any defective tool or equipment (do not repair or discard)
Manufacturer name and model number of involved equipment
Maintenance logs for the equipment (request via attorney)
Safety data sheets for any hazardous materials involved
Contractor and subcontractor information for the job site
Emergency room and all follow-up records
Records of any workers' comp payments received

timelineRisks.risk: Scene is altered and evidence is lost
timelineRisks.action: Construction sites change rapidly. Document everything before the site is modified, equipment is repaired, or scaffolding is dismantled.

timelineRisks.risk: Workers' comp reporting deadline at risk
timelineRisks.action: California and Arizona workers' compensation systems require prompt employer notification. Delay can complicate or jeopardize your claim.
OSHA may investigate serious construction accidents. Cooperate with investigators and preserve your own documentation.
California requires filing a workers' compensation claim within 1 year of injury. Arizona has a 1-year filing deadline for most workers' comp claims.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for third-party personal injury claims
timelineRisks.action: If a third party (contractor, equipment manufacturer) is liable, you generally have 2 years to file a civil claim. Workers' comp does not prevent third-party claims.

insuranceIssues.explanation: Workers' compensation typically covers medical costs and lost wages but limits total recovery. If a third party (general contractor, equipment manufacturer, property owner) contributed to the accident, a separate civil claim may be available.
Workers classified as independent contractors may not be covered by the employer's workers' comp. However, misclassification is common and may be challenged.
Construction site injuries may involve the general contractor, multiple subcontractors, property owners, equipment manufacturers, and architects. Identifying all parties is complex but consequential.
If a defective tool or machine caused the injury, the manufacturer may be liable under product liability laws — separate from and in addition to any workers' comp claim.
Documented OSHA violations at the time of the accident can support both workers' comp claims and third-party lawsuits by establishing negligence.

whenToGetLawyer: Your injuries required surgery, hospitalization, or long-term medical care
A third party (contractor, equipment maker, property owner) may have contributed to the accident
Your employer is disputing the workers' comp claim or your employment status
OSHA violations were present at the time of the accident
You have been offered a workers' comp settlement
Your injuries have caused permanent disability or significant lost wages
